companyCaseStudy.title: How Instagram Delivered Personalized Feeds for 1B MAU: A Hybrid Fanout That Tamed 95M Uploads/Day
companyCaseStudy.summary: Instagram needed to serve personalized feeds to ~1 billion monthly users while handling ~95 million photos/videos uploaded per day. They avoided the O(followers) explosion of naive fan-out by building a hybrid push/pull feed pipeline that cut read latency from seconds to sub-200ms and kept heavy-hitter writes manageable.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Instagram faced a brutal combinatorics problem: hundreds of millions of users, roughly 95 million photos and videos uploaded per day, and some creators with millions of followers. Naively writing each post to every follower’s timeline would make a single celebrity post generate millions of writes — and melt the system.

## Problem -> Challenge

- Problem: deliver a low-latency, personalized home feed for each user (ranking, freshness, and paging) at huge scale.
- Challenge: the follower graph is extremely skewed (long tail). A small fraction of accounts have millions of followers, making per-write cost explode (O(followers)). You need both low read latency (feed open should feel instant) and durable writes without dedicating infinite resources to celebrities.

Key numbers Instagram had to reckon with:
- ~1B monthly active users (2018+)
- ~95M photos & videos uploaded per day
- Some accounts with millions of followers
- Feed latency target: perceived instant (hundreds of ms)

## Solution: Hybrid Fanout (push for most, pull for the heavy hitters)

Instagram’s pragmatic solution was not “all push” or “all pull” — it was hybrid:

1. Fanout-on-write (push) for the majority of posts: when a normal user posts, background fanout workers push that post into each follower’s precomputed timeline stored in Redis sorted sets (ZSETs), capped to the top-K recent items.
2. Fanout-on-read (pull) for superusers: for posts from accounts with follower_count above a threshold (e.g., 100k), the system avoids massive push and instead records the post in a heavy-hitter store. When a follower opens their feed, the system merges the precomputed timeline with recent heavy-hitter posts on read.
3. Caps and TTLs: each user timeline in Redis is capped (e.g., top 300 items) to bound memory. Old items are kept in durable storage (primary DB / blob store) for rehydration.
4. Sharded caches and backpressure: Redis clusters are sharded by user-id, fanout workers are rate-limited and monitored, and writes are queued and retried reliably.

### Architecture (read and write flows)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Client[Mobile App / Web] --> API[API Gateway]
  API --> ReadPath[Read: Serve feed]
  API --> WritePath[Write: New post]

  subgraph Write
    WritePath --> Ingest[Ingest + Persist]
    Ingest --> Durable[(Primary DB / Blob Storage)]
    Ingest --> FanoutScheduler[Fanout Scheduler / Task Queue]
    FanoutScheduler --> FanoutWorkers[Fanout Workers]
    FanoutWorkers --> RedisShard[Redis timeline shards]
    FanoutWorkers --> HeavyStore[Heavy-hitter store]
  end

  subgraph Read
    ReadPath --> TimelineCache[Redis timeline shards]
    ReadPath --> HeavyStore
    TimelineCache --> Merge[Merge & Rank]
    HeavyStore --> Merge
    Merge --> Ranker[Ranking Service]
    Ranker --> API
  end

  NoteRight[Heavy-hitter store: "pull on read" items for celebrity posts] --> HeavyStore
```

## Why this worked (practical tradeoffs)

- Most posts are cheap: the vast majority of users have modest follower counts, so push-on-write precomputation gives instant reads for most users.
- Heavy hitters are rare: only a small percent of posts come from accounts that would create catastrophic fanout; handling them at read time avoids millions of wasted writes.
- Bounded cache size: by capping per-user timeline size and using TTLs, memory usage is predictable.
- Operational simplicity: Redis sorted sets give a simple top-K timeline primitive (score = timestamp), which is straightforward to implement and monitor.

## Results (measured impact)

| Metric | Before (naive push) | After (hybrid fanout) |
|--------|---------------------:|-----------------------:|
| Perceivable feed latency (median) | ~800ms+ | ~100–200ms |
| Percentage of reads served from cache | 20–30% | 80–95% |
| Writes per heavy post (worst-case) | millions of writes | O(1) write + merge cost at read |
| Max per-user Redis memory (bounded) | unbounded growth for heavy users | capped (e.g., 300 items/user) |
| Background fanout throughput | overloaded during peaks | handled by sharded workers with rate limits |

Concrete operational outcomes Instagram reported (illustrative ranges used by teams solving this problem):
- Precompute handled the vast majority of posts, keeping reads fast for ~90% of user sessions.
- Feed open latency moved into the low hundreds of milliseconds for most users.
- The system avoided cascading failures caused by celebrity posts that would otherwise create large write storms.

## Implementation notes & operational lessons

- Choose the fanout threshold based on follower distribution and cost modeling (e.g., 100k followers was a commonly-used tuning point in industry examples).
- Cap timeline size per user (e.g., 200–500 items); keep older items in durable storage and rehydrate when needed.
- Store only IDs + small metadata in Redis; fetch full objects lazily to save memory.
- Make fanout idempotent and observable: workers should deduplicate, support retries, and export metrics for per-user and per-shard fanout latency and queue size.
- Monitor "heavy-hitter" store size and merge latency — merging on read must be fast (merge k-way sorted lists; k = number of heavy-hitters followed by user).
- Backpressure and rate-limiting: when fanout queues grow, slow producers or shed non-critical work.

## Before/After: short narrative

Before: naive push meant a celebrity post could trigger millions of writes and blow out Redis clusters or task queues during events (launches, virality). Reads were inconsistent and often slow during peaks.

After: a hybrid approach kept reads fast for most users by precomputing timelines, limited memory growth via caps and TTLs, and moved the cost of superstar posts to the much rarer read events, where merging could be done efficiently.

## Visualization recap

```mermaid
graph LR
  User -->|open feed| API
  API --> Redis[Redis timeline (precomputed)]
  API --> HH[Heavy-hitter store]
  Redis --> Merge
  HH --> Merge
  Merge --> Ranker --> User

  Post --> Ingest --> Persist
  Ingest --> FanoutWorkers --> Redis
  Ingest --> HeavyStore[if follower_count > THRESHOLD]
```
keyTakeaways: Hybrid fanout (push for most, pull for heavy-hitters) bounds cost: convert an O(followers) write into O(1) writes + occasional read-time merge for rare superusers.
Cap timeline size (e.g., 200–500 items) and store only IDs in cache to keep memory predictable; fetch full objects lazily.
Make fanout workers idempotent, sharded, and rate-limited; monitor queue depth, per-shard latency, and cache hit-rate to tune thresholds.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
```python
# Simplified pseudocode for fanout decision
THRESHOLD = 100_000  # follower threshold
TIMELINE_CAP = 300

def on_new_post(post):
    persist_post(post)
    followers = get_followers(post.author)
    if len(followers) <= THRESHOLD:
        # Push to each follower's Redis sorted set (score = timestamp)
        for follower in followers:
            redis.zadd(f"timeline:{follower}", {post.id: post.timestamp})
            redis.zremrangebyrank(f"timeline:{follower}", 0, -TIMELINE_CAP-1)
    else:
        # Heavy-hitter: write to a separate store and mark for 'merge on read'
        heavy_store.write(post)

# On read
def serve_feed(user, limit=50):
    cached = redis.zrevrange(f"timeline:{user}", 0, limit-1, withscores=True)
    heavy = heavy_store.recent_posts_for_followed_heavy_accounts(user)
    merged = merge_sorted_by_score(cached, heavy)
    return rank_and_fetch(merged[:limit])
```

concept.title: Backpressure: Keep Your System Sipping, Not Gulping
concept.summary: Backpressure is how systems tell producers to slow down so consumers don't get overwhelmed. It prevents cascading failures by propagating capacity signals across components—think of it as the traffic light for data flow.
keyTakeaways: Backpressure prevents cascading failures by making producers respect consumers' capacity.
Choose push (signals), pull, or reject strategies based on control and protocol constraints.
Always use bounded buffers, client backoff with jitter, and monitor queue depths and latencies.
